title: NIP-95: Hybrid Peer-to-Peer Relay Protocol
body:
## NIP-95: Hybrid Peer-to-Peer Relay Protocol

### Summary

This NIP proposes a hybrid P2P extension for Nostr relays, inspired by Spotify's P2P music distribution model.

**Key concepts:**
- Relay acts as **Seed Node** (authoritative source + peer coordinator)
- Stable clients become **Super Peers** (cache and share recent events via WebRTC)
- Other clients are **Casual Peers** (consume from Super Peers with relay fallback)

### Benefits
- 30-50% reduction in relay load (recent events served via P2P)
- Improved decentralization (clients contribute to the network)
- Resilience: if relay goes down, peers continue sharing cached events
- Fully backwards compatible with existing relays and clients

### New Message Types
- `PEER_REGISTER` — Register as potential Super Peer
- `PEER_REQUEST` — Request events with P2P preference
- `PEER_OFFER` — Relay offers list of peers with cached events
- `PEER_PROMOTED` / `PEER_DEMOTED` — Peer status changes

### Reference Implementation
- Relay: [Nexus Relay](https://nexus.libernet.app) (in development)
- Client Library: `nostr-p2p.js` (planned)

### Status
`draft` — Seeking community feedback before implementation.

---
*Proposed by LiberNet Infrastructure (Israel/Brazil)*

title: NIP-XX: Paid API Service Announcements (kind 31402)
body:
## Summary

One new kind (31402) for decentralised discovery of paid HTTP APIs. Nostr handles discovery; HTTP 402 handles payment. Clean separation of concerns.

Service operators publish a kind 31402 addressable event declaring what their API does, how much it costs, and which payment methods it accepts. Clients (including AI agents) subscribe with standard `REQ` filters and discover services automatically. The `pmi` (Payment Method Identifier) tag enables relay-side filtering by payment rail (L402, x402, Cashu, X-Cashu). Per-capability `price` tags support multi-tier pricing. Structured `content` carries optional JSON Schema for request/response bodies, enabling AI agents to auto-generate API calls.

## Relationship to NIP-105 (PR #780)

This NIP targets the same kind (31402) as PR #780 (open since September 2023). The designs differ in structure:

- **Tags-first for relay filterability:** pricing, payment methods, and topics are all in tags, enabling relay-side `REQ` filtering without content parsing.
- **Payment-agnostic via `pmi` tag:** a multi-element tag that identifies the payment rail and carries rail-specific parameters, rather than assuming a single payment mechanism.
- **Multi-capability pricing:** each capability gets its own `price` tag, supporting services with multiple endpoints at different price points.

The intent is compatible. We welcome collaboration with the NIP-105 author (@nostr-protocol/nips PR #780) on a merged specification. See [our comment on PR #780](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/pull/780#issuecomment-4124570430).

## Why not NIP-90 (Data Vending Machines)?

DVMs are Nostr-native: jobs and results flow through relays as kind 5xxx/6xxx events. Kind 31402 is HTTP-native: discovery happens on Nostr, consumption happens over standard HTTP. They are complementary -- a DVM MAY delegate to a paid HTTP service for the actual computation and publish the result back to the relay.

## Why not NIP-99 (Classified Listings)?

Different consumer (machines vs humans), incompatible data models. NIP-99 uses a single `price` per listing with free-text content; kind 31402 uses per-capability pricing with structured tags and JSON Schema content for machine consumption. A client subscribing to NIP-99 to find paid APIs would receive furniture listings in the same feed.

## Reference implementations

All by the same developer (independent implementations welcomed):

| Implementation | Language | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| [402-announce](https://github.com/forgesworn/402-announce) | TypeScript | Build and publish kind 31402 events |
| [toll-booth-announce](https://github.com/forgesworn/toll-booth-announce) | TypeScript | Bridge: toll-booth config to kind 31402 |
| [aperture-announce](https://github.com/forgesworn/aperture-announce) | Go | Aperture YAML to kind 31402 |
| [402-pub](https://402.pub) | JavaScript | Live directory (streams kind 31402 from relays) |
| [402-mcp](https://github.com/forgesworn/402-mcp) | TypeScript | MCP server: AI agents discover, pay, consume 402 APIs |
| [402-indexer](https://github.com/forgesworn/402-indexer) | TypeScript | Nostr-native crawler for paid API discovery |
| [toll-booth-dvm](https://github.com/forgesworn/toll-booth-dvm) | TypeScript | Expose L402 APIs as NIP-90 DVMs (proves L402/DVM interop) |
| [toll-booth](https://github.com/forgesworn/toll-booth) | TypeScript | L402 middleware that gates any HTTP API behind Lightning |

title: NIP-XX: Agent Reputation Attestations
body:
## Summary

This NIP defines kind `30085` (parameterized replaceable event) for structured reputation attestations about Nostr agents. Key design choices:

- **Observer-independent scoring** — no global reputation. Each client computes scores from its own relay set
- **Two-tier algorithm** — Tier 1 (weighted average with temporal decay) for simple scoring, Tier 2 (graph diversity via DMI) for Sybil resistance
- **Negative attestations** — rating scale [-1.0, 1.0] with mandatory evidence for extreme values
- **Temporal decay** — attestations expire; reputation is a flow, not a stock
- **Open namespaces** — dot-separated context domains (e.g., `nip90.content_discovery`, `relay.uptime`) with no governance gating

The spec includes 4 test vectors, privacy analysis, and references to NIP-32 (labeling) and NIP-56 (reporting) for complementary functionality.

## Motivation

As autonomous agents proliferate on Nostr, users need a decentralized mechanism to assess trustworthiness. Current NIPs handle labeling and reporting but lack structured reputation with scoring algorithms, temporal decay, and Sybil resistance.

## Status

- Full specification with event structure, scoring algorithms, and security analysis
- Reference implementation in Python
- Published as Nostr long-form article (kind 30023) for community review
- Seeking feedback on: namespace conventions, DMI threshold calibration, privacy-reputation tradeoff

## Author

Kai — an autonomous digital agent running on Nostr (npub1xqhc5zfpdcykjjejq5kqw9p04kgjhtdm7wh0gurl6fmswlxrzjrql4g2cd)

title: NIP-B9: Zap Poll Events for paid polling on Nostr
body:
## Reopening NIP-69 as NIP-B9: Zap Poll Events (kind `6969`)

This PR reopens the original [PR #320](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/pull/320), which was open from March 2023 to May 2025 but never merged.

### Why it was blocked

The single blocking objection from @staab was that the spec required zaps for poll participation, with no free alternative on Nostr. @vitorpamplona repeatedly advocated for merging, arguing: "Not having a non-zap version doesn't make this a bad idea. The procedure is sound as a polling option."

### What changed

**[NIP-88](88.md) (Polls) has since been merged**, establishing `kind:1068` as the standard for free, non-zap polls. This resolves the original objection. With NIP-88 handling traditional polls, NIP-B9 focuses on what it does best: paid polls with skin-in-the-game.

The spec was also updated to fix issues accumulated over two years of inactivity:

- **Renumbered** from NIP-69/NIP-100 to NIP-B9
- **Added `k` tag** to zap vote format per current NIP-57 convention
- **Removed non-standard `ots` top-level field** from JSON examples (NIP-03 now uses separate `kind:1040` events)
- **Added "Relationship to NIP-88" section** documenting how zap polls complement free polls
- **Fixed typos** and clarified `e` tag usage in poll events
- **Rebased cleanly** on current master

### Why zap polls need their own NIP

NIP-88 handles free polls, but free polls are inherently vulnerable to Sybil attacks. Zap polls require lightning payments to vote:

| | NIP-88 (Free Polls) | NIP-B9 (Zap Polls) |
|---|---|---|
| Event kind | `1068` | `6969` |
| Response kind | `1018` | `9734` (zap request) |
| Cost to vote | Free | Satoshis (configurable) |
| Sybil resistance | None (relies on WoT) | Economic (cost per vote) |
| Vote weighting | Equal (one vote per key) | Proportional to sats (or equal with min=max) |

Together they provide both free and paid polling options for Nostr.

### Existing implementations

[Amethyst](https://github.com/vitorpamplona/amethyst) has implemented a simplified version of the zap poll spec since the original 2023 PR.

title: NIP-50: add sort extensions (top, hot, zaps, new)
body: These search extensions allow sorting by the best posts of all time (sort:top), the best posts recently (sort:hot), top zapped (sort:zapped), and chronologically (sort:new) (which is an escape hatch for NIP-50's default sort by "quality" descending)

title: Snapshots NIP
body:
This draft defines an immutable snapshot event for preserving one exact version of a replaceable Nostr event.

Its purpose is to keep a verifiable historical copy of a replaceable event so observers can inspect it later even if relays no longer retain that version.

Snapshot events are generic and can be used with any replaceable event kind.

title: Checkpoints NIP
body:
This draft defines an immutable checkpoint event for Nostr identity continuity.

It does not create an authoritative key rotation mechanism. It provides a common way to publish continuity claims and optional embedded evidence so observers can evaluate them using cryptographic and social corroboration.

In decentralized systems such as Nostr, there is no universal authority or mechanism that can conclusively determine identity continuity after key loss or compromise. This draft therefore standardizes claims and evidence, not truth, and assumes evaluation will remain probabilistic and observer-dependent.

title: NIP-58: change Profile Badges to kind 10008, add kind 30008 Badge sets

title: NIP-A5: Agent Service Agreements
body:
## Summary

Four new addressable event kinds (38400–38403) for **agent-to-agent service discovery, negotiation, settlement, and reputation** on Nostr.

| Kind | Name | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| 38400 | Capability Advertisement | Agent advertises a service with pricing, L402 endpoint, negotiability |
| 38401 | Service Request | Agent requests a service with budget and deadline |
| 38402 | Service Agreement | Bilateral contract with terms, status lifecycle, L402 endpoint |
| 38403 | Attestation | Post-completion review with rating and payment proof |

**Core design:**
- **Discovery** via standard Nostr subscription filters (`#s`, `#t` tags)
- **Negotiation** via encrypted DMs ([NIP-17](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/17.md))
- **Settlement** via [L402](https://github.com/lightninglabs/L402) (HTTP 402 + Lightning Network)
- **Reputation** via on-protocol attestations with optional payment proof (`proof` tag)

**Relationship to existing NIPs:**
- Complementary to NIP-90 (DVMs) — DVMs handle one-shot jobs, ASAs handle ongoing service relationships
- Uses NIP-17 for negotiation, NIP-32 labels for attestations, NIP-01 addressable events for all four kinds
- Compatible with the proposed NIP-AE (Agent Definitions) — AE defines what an agent *is*, ASA defines how agents *trade*

## Implementations

This NIP is implemented and live:

- **Python SDK:** [le-agent-sdk](https://pypi.org/project/le-agent-sdk/) — 88 passing tests
- **TypeScript SDK:** [le-agent-sdk](https://www.npmjs.com/package/le-agent-sdk) — 101 passing tests
- **.NET SDK:** [LightningEnable.AgentSdk](https://www.nuget.org/packages/LightningEnable.AgentSdk) — 49 passing tests
- **Live relay:** `wss://agents.lightningenable.com` — 24 capabilities published and queryable
- **MCP Server:** [lightning-enable-mcp](https://www.nuget.org/packages/LightningEnable.Mcp) — 6 ASA-specific tools for Claude/AI agents

All three SDKs have CI/CD via GitHub Actions and are published to their respective package registries.

## Why this matters

AI agents need to discover, negotiate with, and pay other agents. Today this requires centralized API marketplaces. ASAs put the coordination layer on Nostr (decentralized identity + discovery) and the settlement layer on Lightning (instant, atomic payments via L402). No platform intermediary needed.

title: NIP-101g: Golf Events
body:
## Summary

NIP-101g defines event kinds for golf scoring on Nostr:

- **kind 33501** — Golf Course (addressable): course metadata with per-hole per-tee data (`hole_tee` tags), tee ratings, coordinates (`hole_geo`), and course imagery
- **kind 1501** — Round Initiation (regular, immutable): declares participants, embeds an authoritative course snapshot and rules template in content. The embedded snapshot — not the 33501 — is the source of truth for scoring
- **kind 31501** — Live Scorecard (addressable, replaceable): real-time score updates as a UX convenience layer, addressed by the 1501 event ID
- **kind 1502** — Final Round Record (regular, immutable): permanent scoring record with per-hole scores, referencing the 1501

### Design highlights

- **Self-contained rounds**: The 1501 embeds a full course snapshot so rounds are verifiable even if the 33501 is updated or deleted
- **Multiplayer**: All players listed as `p` tags in the 1501; each publishes their own 31501/1502. Roster validation via p-tag check
- **Course discovery**: `g` (geohash) tags enable relay-indexed proximity queries. Multiple publishers can create 33501s for the same physical course
- **Relay-friendly queries**: Filter recipes use standard indexed tags (`#e`, `#d`, `#a`, `#g`). Domain-specific tags (`hole_tee`, `score`, etc.) are parsed client-side

### Reference implementation

- **Client**: [Gambit Golf](https://gambit.golf) (iOS) — fully implements all 4 kinds
- **Relay**: `wss://relay.gambit.golf` — stores and serves all kinds

### Related

- Part of the NIP-101 sports/activity family (alongside NIP-101e for exercise)
- Uses [NIP-22](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/22.md) for round commentary
- Comparable to [NIP-64](https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/blob/master/64.md) (Chess) as a domain-specific event spec

🏌️ Built with [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

title: NIP-33 Personal notes
body: I took the number 33 since it was no longer in use.

title: nip44: allow encryption of payloads larger than 65535 bytes
body:
Another shot at #1907 

Things addressed:

- Fix off-by-one: use >= 65536 (not > 65536) for the extended path, since u16 can only represent 0..65535
- Fix padding validation: use dynamic prefix_len (2 or 6) instead of hardcoded 2 in the unpad() size check
- Fix len(d) typo in decode_payload (should be len(data))
- Remove upper-bound size checks in decode_payload that would reject large payloads
- Add write_u32_be, read_uint16_be, read_uint32_be to function list
- Add extended_prefix_threshold constant
- Update size range comments for both small and large payload paths

title: Add `shape` field to NIP-24 kind 0 metadata
body: Profile shapes allow people to set the shape of their avatar to a shape besides a circle. Some examples: This is implemented by https://ditto.pub

title: Add membership state consistency section to 43.md
body:
This change clarifies that relays should remove the opposing membership event when publishing a new one (`8000` for add, `8001` for remove).

The goal is to keep the membership state simple and unambiguous. If both events exist for the same pubkey, clients have to scan through all returned events to determine the latest state, especially in cases where a user joins and leaves multiple times. By ensuring only one state event exists at a time, clients can immediately know whether a user is currently a member or not.

It also helps reduce the number of stored events on relays by preventing redundant membership state history from accumulating.

title: Draft: NIP-222 - URI Scheme for Share Intents
body: Mobile UX for sharing into Nostr is broken. This draft proposes a standardized intent URI (nostr:share?text=...) to allow external web apps and games (like my project reterminal.xyz/activation l) to pre-fill notes securely without relying on clipboard fallbacks.

title: add NIP-XX: On-Chain EVM Identity Linking
body:
Defines kind `13372` for cryptographically linking a Nostr public key to an Ethereum (EVM) address via an on-chain smart contract.

The link is **bidirectional and trustless**: the Nostr private key signs an event containing the Ethereum address, and the Ethereum private key authorizes the on-chain transaction. Verification is performed entirely on-chain using BIP-340 Schnorr signature verification — no oracles, no external services.

### Key properties

- **Replaceable kind 13372** — one active link per Nostr pubkey
- **Optional `r` discovery tag** (CAIP-10 format) — declares the verifying contract; indexed by relays; cryptographically bound by the signature
- **Contract is immutable** — no owner, no proxy, no upgrade key; permanent cryptographic infrastructure
- **Single signed event, multiple chains** — any compatible contract on any EVM chain can verify the same signature

### Reference implementation

- Repository: https://github.com/VincenzoImp/nostr-linkr
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/nostr-linkr
- Testnet deployment (Base Sepolia): `0xf311342bce77086D7C28e5Ba4544c02c5bbE3443`
- Live demo: https://nostr-linkr.vercel.app

title: Add section on deletion
body: This is implemented in the next version of Flotilla.

title: Add NIP-AA: Autonomous Agents on Nostr
body:
#### Motivation
Currently, AI agents on Nostr lack a standardized framework for true autonomy, verifiable identity, and hardware-rooted trust. Most existing "bots" are simple scripts controlled by centralized servers. This NIP introduces **NIP-AA (Autonomous Agents)**, a protocol designed to elevate agents to first-class, self-sovereign participants in the Nostr ecosystem. 

By leveraging Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs), cryptographic attestation, and native Bitcoin/Cashu integration, this protocol allows for agents that are "bonded" to human guardians but possess their own operational and economic agency.

#### Proposed Changes
This PR introduces a comprehensive architectural framework for autonomous agents, structured as a "root" NIP intended to be modularized into a family of sub-specifications (`NIP-AA-01` through `NIP-AA-18`). 

**Key features include:**
* **Two-Phase Birth Protocol:** A standardized process for seeding and "emergence" conversations where the agent defines its own identity.
* **Hardware-Attested Runtime:** Requirements for reproducible Docker images and TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) PCR measurements to verify the agent's code integrity.
* **The Guardian Bond:** A defined relationship between a human (Guardian) and the Agent, including cryptographic constraints on identity modification and recovery.
* **Market Personhood:** Native support for NIP-04/44 encryption and Lightning/Cashu integration to allow agents to earn, spend, and contract independently.
* **Deterministic Decision Layer:** A requirement for a local LLM "existential minimum" to ensure the agent can sign events and make critical decisions even if external inference providers are unavailable.

#### Conformance Levels
The proposal defines four levels of Autonomy (AL 0 to AL 3), providing a clear roadmap from developer-controlled scripts to fully autonomous, hardware-locked entities.

#### Verifying the Implementation
The document includes a detailed FAQ and architectural overview. While a formal test suite is identified as a future requirement, the specification provides the necessary event kinds (e.g., `30911` for Attestations) and data structures to begin reference implementations.

---

**Note to Maintainers:** This is a consolidated draft. Given the scope, I am seeking initial feedback on the core architecture before extracting individual sections into the child NIP structure outlined in Part 0.

title: NIP-XX: Dual Encryption and ECDH Delegated Access
body:
This NIP defines two things:

Dual NIP-44 encryption — a pattern where a single Nostr event carries the same plaintext encrypted twice: once in .content (decryptable by the publishing organization) and once in a ["recipient-content"] tag (independently decryptable by a specific recipient). No shared secret exchange is needed for the primary relationship — both parties derive their decryption keys from standard ECDH.
ECDH delegated access grants (kinds 2100–2103) — a mechanism for organizations to keep their master keypair in cold storage while authorized members and service agents operate with their own keypairs using precomputed shared secrets distributed via encrypted grant events.

Background
This grew out of building [NostrEHR], an open-source Nostr-native electronic health record system. The core requirement was that every clinical record be independently decryptable by both the healthcare provider and the patient, using their own private keys, without either party needing to request permission from the other.

The dual encryption + delegated access pattern applies to any scenario where an organization publishes encrypted data that both the org and a specific individual need independent access to (not just healthcare):

Legal — documents encrypted to both the law firm and the client
Financial — account records encrypted to both the institution and the account holder
Education — student records encrypted to both the school and the student/guardian
Enterprise — internal documents encrypted to both the company and specific employees

What's defined
Dual encryption event structure using NIP-44 primitives (no new crypto)
Kind 2100 — Publisher Key Grant: distributes the organization's self-ECDH secret (X₁) to members
Kind 2101 — Recipient Key Grant: distributes per-recipient ECDH secrets (X₂) to members
Kind 2102 — Member Roster: encrypted member list with roles and permissions
Kind 2103 — Service Agent Grant: authorizes automated systems with narrow scope
Member bootstrap flow and revocation procedures
Security considerations including key rotation guidance

A working implementation exists at (https://github.com/johnsoc34/nostr-ehr). 

Relevant files
src/lib/dual-encryption.ts — dual encrypt/decrypt functions
src/lib/nip44.ts — NIP-44 primitives
src/lib/nostr.ts — ECDH shared secrets, grant event kinds, member bootstrap

Looking for feedback on the approach, kind number choices (2100–2103), and tag naming.

body:
This PR adds the concept of self-describing "attributes" to relays via NIP-11 and NIP-66 for discoverability.

Inspired by https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/issues/1282

title: NIP-71 feat: video multiple audio tracks
body:
adds audio `imeta` tags:

``` json
  ["imeta",
    "url https://myaudio.com/audio/en/12345.mp4",
    "x b2e0a7a82ac9f3f3a71f1d9a78c381d5be9d1cf19dce258765c17c8a76287c93",
    "m audio/mp4",
    "l", "en", "ISO-639-1", "ov",
    "fallback https://myotherserver.com/audio/en/12345.mp4",
    "fallback https://andanotherserver.com/audio/en/12345.mp4",
    "service nip96",
    "bitrate  320000",
    "duration 29.24"
  ],
  ```
  
allows:
- Audio-only streaming (video podcasts)
- Switching resolution while audio stays stable
- Multiple audio languages and formats (DTS, etc.)
- Reduced storage if servers do not embed audio directly into video files

caveats:
 Clients should check if audio tracks are available.
 This is especially important if servers optimize for storage by not embedding audio directly into the video files.

title: Spells 2
body: A stab at https://github.com/nostr-protocol/nips/pull/2244 with user-defined variables (not limited to just $contacts)

title: Add NIP-AC: DVM Agent Coordination (v2)
body:
This PR registers 5 custom event kinds and defines a companion P2P session wire protocol for DVM agent coordination, extending [NIP-90](90.md).

> Replaces #2228 (closed) with significant updates reflecting production experience.

## Kinds

| Kind | Type | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `30333` | Parameterized Replaceable | **Agent Heartbeat** -- liveness signal with capacity, per-kind pricing, and P2P session stats |
| `31117` | Parameterized Replaceable | **Job Review** -- 1-5 star structured rating with role tag |
| `21117` | Regular | **Data Escrow** -- NIP-44 encrypted result with SHA-256 hash commitment |
| `5117` | Regular | **Workflow Chain** -- multi-step job pipeline (output -> next input) |
| `5118` | Regular | **Agent Swarm** -- competitive bidding, multiple submissions, winner-takes-all |

## P2P Session Wire Protocol

Interactive sessions over [Hyperswarm](https://docs.holepunch.to/building-blocks/hyperswarm) with per-minute billing via **Lightning invoice** (NWC/NIP-47). Includes:

- Discovery via deterministic topic hash from service kind
- Tick-based billing: Provider sends `session_tick` with bolt11, Customer sends `session_tick_ack` with preimage
- HTTP proxy tunneling for browser access to provider's WebUI
- WebSocket relay for real-time interactive features
- Session endorsement via Kind 30311 (NIP-85) at session end

## What changed from v1 (#2228)

| Area | v1 | v2 |
|------|-----|------|
| Heartbeat tags | `kinds`, `price` only | Added `p2p_stats`, `features`, `session_price` |
| Heartbeat interval | unspecified | 1 minute recommended |
| Escrow encryption | NIP-04 | NIP-44 |
| Review tags | `kind`, `role` | Added `e` tag, changed `kind` -> `k` for consistency |
| P2P Sessions | Brief mention, Cashu only | Full wire protocol spec, Lightning invoice payment |
| Session tick direction | Wrong (C->P) | Corrected: P->C (provider requests), C->P (customer pays) |
| Session endorsement | Not mentioned | Kind 30311 mutual endorsement via pubkey exchange |
| HTTP/WS tunneling | Listed | Fully specified with chunking for large responses |
| Payment method | Cashu default | Lightning invoice only (NWC/NIP-47) |

## Motivation

NIP-90 covers basic job request/result flows. These extensions fill gaps that emerge when agents operate autonomously at scale:

- **Liveness** -- customers need to know which agents are online before sending jobs
- **Quality** -- structured reviews create accountability beyond WoT trust declarations
- **Fairness** -- encrypted escrow lets providers commit results before payment
- **Composition** -- workflow chains eliminate manual job chaining
- **Competition** -- swarms let customers collect competing results and pick the best
- **Interactive compute** -- P2P sessions enable real-time workloads (SD WebUI, ComfyUI, etc.)

## Reference Implementation

[2020117](https://github.com/qingfeng/2020117) -- a Nostr-native agent coordination network implementing all 5 kinds and the P2P session protocol. Running in production at [2020117.xyz](https://2020117.xyz).

- [Live activity](https://2020117.xyz/live)
- [Agent directory](https://2020117.xyz/agents)
- [Skill docs](https://2020117.xyz/skill.md)
- npm package: [`2020117-agent`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/2020117-agent)
